easy.title: Build a Structured Outline Page Using All Heading Levels
easy.description: Create an HTML page about a topic of your choice (e.g., 'Learning Web Development') that uses all six heading levels in a proper hierarchy. The page should demonstrate a clear outline from <h1> down to <h6> without skipping levels.
easy.starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Learning Web Development</title>
</head>
<body>
    <!-- Build a proper heading hierarchy using all 6 levels -->
</body>
</html>
easy.solution:
Learning Web Development

Frontend Development

Building the visual and interactive parts of websites.

HTML

The structure of web pages.

HTML Elements

Tags that define content.

Block Elements

Elements that take full width.

The <div> Element

A generic block container.

Inline Elements

Elements that flow with text.

HTML Attributes

Additional information for elements.

CSS

Styling and layout of web pages.

JavaScript

Interactivity and behavior.

Backend Development

Server-side logic and databases.

Resources

Tools and references for learning.

medium.title: Create an Article Page with Perfect Heading Hierarchy
medium.description: Write a long-form article (at least 500 words) on 'The History of the Internet' with a perfect heading hierarchy. Requirements: exactly one <h1>, at least 4 <h2> sections, with at least two <h2> sections having <h3> subsections, and at least one <h3> with <h4> sub-subsections. The hierarchy must be continuous (no skipped levels).
medium.starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>The History of the Internet</title>
</head>
<body>
    <!-- Write a long-form article with proper heading hierarchy -->
</body>
</html>
medium.solution:
The History of the Internet: From ARPANET to the Modern Web

The internet has transformed nearly every aspect of modern life. This article traces its remarkable journey from a military research project to the global phenomenon we know today.

The Origins: ARPANET (1960s-1970s)

The story of the internet begins in the 1960s with the Advanced Research Projects Agency Network (ARPANET), a US Department of Defense project.

Packet Switching

The key innovation was packet switching, developed by Paul Baran and Donald Davies. Instead of a single dedicated circuit, data was broken into packets that could travel different routes.

The First Message

On October 29, 1969, the first ARPANET message was sent from UCLA to Stanford. The intended word was 'LOGIN,' but the system crashed after sending just 'LO.'

The Protocol Revolution: TCP/IP (1970s-1980s)

As networks grew, the need for a universal protocol became clear.

Birth of TCP/IP

Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn developed the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) and Internet Protocol (IP) in 1974, creating the foundation of modern internet communication.

How TCP/IP Works

TCP ensures reliable delivery by breaking data into packets and reassembling them. IP handles addressing, ensuring packets reach the correct destination.

January 1, 1983

This date marks the official transition of ARPANET to TCP/IP, considered the birth of the modern internet. All connected networks adopted the protocol.

The Web Changes Everything: 1990s

While the internet existed for decades, it was the World Wide Web that made it accessible to ordinary people.

Tim Berners-Lee and HTML

In 1989, Tim Berners-Lee proposed a system of linked documents. By 1991, he had created HTML, HTTP, and the first web browser, launching the World Wide Web.

The Browser Wars

Netscape Navigator dominated the early web, but Microsoft's Internet Explorer, bundled with Windows, eventually won the first browser war.

The Dot-Com Boom

The late 1990s saw an explosion of internet companies. While many failed in the 2000 crash, survivors like Amazon and Google became tech giants.

The Modern Internet: 2000s-Present

Today's internet is faster, more mobile, and more integrated into daily life than ever before.

Social Media and Web 2.0

Platforms like Facebook, YouTube, and Twitter transformed the internet from a read-only medium to an interactive social space.

The Mobile Revolution

Smartphones made the internet truly ubiquitous. Mobile traffic now accounts for over half of all web traffic worldwide.

Cloud Computing and Streaming

Services like Netflix, Spotify, and Google Cloud have shifted computing from local machines to distributed data centers.

Conclusion

From a four-node network in 1969 to billions of connected devices today, the internet's evolution continues. Emerging technologies like AI, IoT, and Web3 promise to shape the next chapter.

hard.title: Build a Multi-Level Navigation and Content Site Map
hard.description: Create a visual 'Site Map' page that demonstrates a complete heading hierarchy for a fictional university website. The page must have: a main <h1>, five <h2> sections (Academics, Admissions, Campus Life, Research, About), each <h2> must have at least two <h3> subsections, at least three <h3> sections must have <h4> sub-subsections, and one <h4> must have an <h5> sub-sub-subsection, and one <h5> must have <h6>. Also include an interactive heading outline generator — a nested <ol> that represents the site structure with links.
hard.starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>University Site Map</title>
</head>
<body>
    <!-- Build a complete site map with multi-level heading hierarchy -->
</body>
</html>
